coredata.dc:title: Observation of room temperature metal free ferromagnetism in sulfur doped graphitic carbon nitride
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
coredata.dc:creator: Majumder, Chinmoy
Kumar Mondal, Tapas
Bhattacharya, Shatabda
Saha, Shyamal K
coredata.dc:description: Generation of superior magnetic property in two-dimensional nanosheets is an important issue in condensed matter physics due to its potential exploitation in the field of Spintronic application. Metal-free ferromagnetism in 2D materials could generate a long spin lifetime due to its s/p electron configuration, which is the key factor for spin transfer. In the present work, we have synthesised 2D graphitic carbon nitride doped with sulfur (S) atom to investigate the magnetic property due to the interaction of the S atom with the lattice of graphitic carbon nitride. Due to S doping, we get a long-range ferromagnetic ordering in our sample, persisting upto room temperature, and the precise control of S concentration can tune the magnetic property of the system. Interestingly, the magnetotransport (MR) study shows a transition from positive MR at low temperature to negative MR at room temperature. The wave function shrinkage model explains the low-temperature positive MR, where the negative MR follows the weak localization effect. Thus, the overall magnetic and electrical response of S doped graphitic carbon nitride makes it a potential candidate for future spintronic application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Erromagnetism
Charge Transfer
Magnetoresistance
Wave function shrinkage
Weak localization
Spintronic

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: C.M. acknowledges Department of Science and Technology (DST)-INSPIRE New Delhi for awarding their fellowships. T.K.M. and S.B would like to thank IACS for awarding Post-doctoral fellowship. We are very thankful to Prof. Dipankar Chakraborty for his valuable suggestions. S.K.S. acknowledges DST, New Delhi, Government of India for the infrastructural facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) The XRD patteren of sample S1, S2 and (c) of pristine g-C3N4. (d) The FT-IR spectra of S doped g-C3N4 (Sample S2).
(a) The TEM structural micrograph of as-synthesised S doped g-C3N4 (Sample S2). (b) and (c) high resolution lattice image. (d) IFFT calculated from the FFT spots in panel.

Element mapping by transmission electron microscopy of sample S2. (a) The portion of the sample where element mapping is performed. Mapping of individual element (b) Carbon (C) (c) Nitrogen (N) (d) Sulfur (S).
(a) The AFM image of pristine g-C3N4. (b) The height profile of selected portion.
(a) The full range XPS spectra of S2. (b) and (c) the high resolution deconvulated XPS spectra of C 1 s and N 1 s. (d) the high resolution XPS spectra of S 2p of S2.
(a) The full range XPS spectra of S1. (b) and (c) the high resolution deconvulated XPS spectra of C 1 s and N 1 s. (d) the high resolution XPS spectra of S 2p of S1.
(a) and (b) The M − H plot of sample S1 and S2 at different temperatures. Inset shows the magnefied fiew of M − H plot at 2 K. (c) and (d) M − T data of sample S1 and S2, respectively.
(a) and (b) The temperature dependent resistivity plot of sample S1 and S2, respectively.
(a) and (b) The magnetoresistance of sample S1 at different temperatures. (c) and (d) The magnetoresistance of sample S2 at different temperatures.
Atomic ratio of C, N, O, and S for S1 and S2 obtained by XPS.
Coercivity values of sample S1 and S2 at different temperatures.
Calculated parameters a0, N(EF), and Rhop at different magnetic fields.
Calculated parameters a0 and Rhop at different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Observation of room temperature metal free ferromagnetism in sulfur doped graphitic carbon nitride

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Sciences, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Jadavpur, Kolkata-700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Sciences, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Jadavpur, Kolkata-700032, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Tapas Kumar Mondal and Shatabda Bhattacharya contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Generation of superior magnetic property in two-dimensional nanosheets is an important issue in condensed matter physics due to its potential exploitation in the field of Spintronic application. Metal-free ferromagnetism in 2D materials could generate a long spin lifetime due to its s/p electron configuration, which is the key factor for spin transfer. In the present work, we have synthesised 2D graphitic carbon nitride doped with sulfur (S) atom to investigate the magnetic property due to the interaction of the S atom with the lattice of graphitic carbon nitride. Due to S doping, we get a long-range ferromagnetic ordering in our sample, persisting upto room temperature, and the precise control of S concentration can tune the magnetic property of the system. Interestingly, the magnetotransport (MR) study shows a transition from positive MR at low temperature to negative MR at room temperature. The wave function shrinkage model explains the low-temperature positive MR, where the negative MR follows the weak localization effect. Thus, the overall magnetic and electrical response of S doped graphitic carbon nitride makes it a potential candidate for future spintronic application.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of high-temperature ferromagnetism in graphene nanoribbons due to modified edge states, immense interest has been devoted in the study of next-generation spintronic devices [1,2]. Due to its low dimensionality, graphene has emerged as a novel electronic material with intriguing electrical properties [3–5]. Therefore, it is anticipated that graphene and its analogous 2D materials could show an advantage in spintronic applications. However, many theoretical and experimental results proved that most of the carbon-based 2D materials are nonmagnetic in nature [6]. Considerable efforts have been given to resolve this issue and theoretical and experimental results showed that room temperature ferromagnetism could be achieved in graphene by defects generation [7,8], hydrogenation [9], and sulfur doping [10,11]. It is important to long spin relaxation time, which enhances the spin–orbit coupling for spintronic device [12]. In s-p electronics system, generation of ferromagnetism without any metal, results weak spin–orbit coupling, which leads to long spin relaxation time in the system [13,14]. Besides this, pure graphene application is limited due to its zero bandgap [15]. In this context, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a carbon-based 2D material with a wide bandgap, is a suitable candidate for various applications [16]. Also, its unique porous structure makes it suitable to dope different atoms to tune the property. Therefore, it is required to generate ferromagnetism in g-C3N4 for future carbon-based material for the spintronic applications.

Interestingly, in recent time g-C3N4 has been applied in different fields like energy conversion, environmental application, photocatalysis, and hydrogen evolution [17,18]. The non-volatile memory effect has made g-C3N4 a potential candidate for spintronic applications. Although, pristine g-C3N4 is diamagnetic in nature with a 2D planner structure [19], theoretical studies have proposed different ways to generate magnetism in the g-C3N4 structure, through defect generation and doping. Among these, chemical doping is a constructive approach to tune the electric and magnetic properties of g-C3N4 system. Recently, few works on the optical and photoelectric applications have been carried out by doping S atom in the g-C3N4 lattice [20,21], and it is easily doped in the lattice and forms a stable structure. In recent time, Tucek al. [11] studied magnetism in S doped graphene due to the interaction of paramagnetic center through the localized ᴨ electrons, and Xu al. [22] studied spin-polarized magnetism in the pure g-C3N4 system. Therefore, it is believed that substitutional doping of S atom in the g-C3N4 lattice results some exciting phenomena in the context of magnetism. We have used this concept to dope S atom in the g-C3N4 lattice. Due to the similar nature of electronegativity of S and N, it is suitable to replace N in g-C3N4 with S, which also supported by theoretical calculation [23].

With this consideration, doping of S atom in g-C3N4 lattice, generates localized spin moments in the g-C3N4 lattice. These spin-carrying centers interact through ᴨ electrons of the system and generate long-range ferromagnetic ordering. In our case, doping percentage highly controls the magnetic property, and below the threshold level, the magnetic ordering in the system is decreased. Our study shows an exciting saturated hysteresis loop with coercivity 315 Oe. Interestingly, upto room temperature, we get hysteresis in the M − H data. The interaction of magnetic moment with the conduction electrons is observed in magnetoresistance measurements (MR), and a transition from positive MR to negative MR at high temperature is observed. The low-temperature MR is explained by the wave function shrinkage model which is supported by electrical transport data, and the weak localization effect explains the negative MR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sulphur doped graphitic carbon nitride
ce:section.ce:para: Sulfur doped graphitic carbon nitride sheets are synthesized from melamine and thiourea by pyrolysis method. We have synthesized two different sulfur doped graphitic carbon nitride sheets (Sample S1 and Sample S2) with different percentage of sulfur doping by varying the thiourea concentration. In this synthesis technique, 0.3 g of thiourea and 0.7 g melamine are mixed in a mortar pestle and the powdered mixture are taken in an alumina boat and placed it in a heating furnace at 550 °C for 5 h under argon atmosphere with heating rate at 5 °C/min. After completion of reaction, it is observed that white powdered mixture is converted into yellow product. The yellow product is then dispersed in distilled water and stirred for 10 min after stirring the supernatant white liquid containing unreacted molecule is removed and the solid material is sonicated further for 4 h. After sonication, the solution is then centrifuged and washed with distilled water followed by ethanol and dried at 65 °C. The dried S doped g-C3N4 sheets (Sample S1) are kept in vacuumed desiccator for further measurements. We have varied the thiourea concentration, keeping melamine amount fixed, synthesised two samples S1 and S2. For the preparation of Sample S2, we have taken 0.5 g of thiourea and 0.7 g of melamine keeping the other process unaltered.

The FT-IR spectra of S-doped graphitic carbon nitride sheets are shown in Fig. 4 (d). A broad absorption peak appeared in the range of 3100–3500 cm−1 for the stretching vibrations of –N-H and –O-H bonds. Peaks at 1585 cm−1 correspond to –C = N bond [24]. The peaks appeared at 1233 and 1405 cm−1 correspond to –C-N stretching vibration of aromatic ring. The peak at 1140 cm−1 appeared due to stretching vibration of -C = S bonds [25]. The intense peak at 805 appeared due to triazine ring mode. A small peak at 635 cm−1 is observed due to -C-S stretching vibration [26].

Powder X-ray diffraction of as-synthesized samples are analysed using an X-ray diffractometer RIGAKU SMARTLAB-XRD, wavelength 1.5418 Å, X-Ray Generator-Cu, 45 kV, 200 mA. The XRD pattern of samples S1 and S2 are shown in Fig. 1 . The two distinct peaks of the g-C3N4, S1 and S2 around 13.1ο and 27.3ο corresponds to the (100) and (002) planes, respectively, signifying the interplanar structural packing and the stacking of the conjugated aromatic system [27]. All the peaks are matched with JCPDS No. 87–1526. Compared to pristine g-C3N4, there is no apparent difference among S1 and S2, which clearly indicates that the crystal structure of g-C3N4 remains unaltered after doping of sulfur. Compared to pristine g-C3N4, the (002) peak of samples S1 and S2 are apparently widened and weakened due to the lattice distortion due to S doping in the lattice of g-C3N4.

The morphology of the nanostructures is further investigated by the field emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM) using a JEOL-2011 high-resolution transmission electron microscope. From TEM micrograph shown in Fig. 2 of sample S2, it is seen that a thin layered 2D sheet-like structure with a more than 300 nm lateral dimension is produced in this synthesis technique. A high-resolution lattice image, shown in Fig. 2 (d), indicates the lattice spacing of 0.32 nm corresponding to the in-plane lattice (002), which is in good agreement with the XRD data. To understand the substitutional doping in the g-C3N4 lattice, we have performed elemental mapping in the sample. The elemental mapping of the sample S2 indicates that S atoms are uniformly dispersed in the layered structure as shown in Fig. 3
. The distribution of the S atoms in the g-C3N4 lattice is clearly visualized from this image.

The thickness of the pristine graphitic carbon nitride is measured by atomic force microscopy measurement in the contact mode by PicoLE AFM equipment (Agilent Corp.,USA). The sample was prepared for measurement by coating thin film on Si/SiO2 wafer (Sigma Aldrich). Fig. 4 (a) is the AFM image of the sample, represents a thin 2D sheet-like morphology, lateral dimension more than 200 nm. From the selected portion of this image, we get the thickness of the 2D graphitic carbon, which is ∼ 2 nm, shown in Fig. 4 (b). The layer separation of g-C3N4 is 0.32 nm which gives the layer number in our case is near about 6, which confirmed the synthesized sample is very thin in nature.

The doping of graphitic carbon nitride with S atoms has been investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using OMICRON-0571 system under ultrahigh vacuum condition. The full range of XPS spectra of sample S2 and S1 are shown in Fig. 5
(a) and 6(a) respectively, from which the presence of C, N, O, and S, with peaks located at binding energies at ∼ 284.5, 398.2, 532.7, and 164.5 eV, typical for C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and S 2p states, respectively, is confirmed. There are no other elements present in the sample except C, N, O and S confirming the purity of the sample. Moreover, impurities of magnetic metals (like Fe, Ni and Co) are also not detected in the sample. In order to detect the bonding nature of the sample we have deconvoluted the C 1 s spectra of the sample S2 and S1, shown in Fig. 5 (b) and Fig. 6
(b), respectively. Three distinct peaks of binding energies at 284.5, 287.6, and 286.4 eV are appeared in sample S2, which is consistent with the binding energy values of sample S1. The peak at 284.6 eV corresponds to sp2 hybridized C-C and 287.6 eV are sp2 hybridized N involved -C-N [28,29]. Moreover, the peak at 286.4 eV is ascribed to the C-S bond due to the doping of S atom in the lattice. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum, shown in Fig. 5 (c) and Fig. 6 (c) are for sample S2 and S1, respectively. In case of sample S2, the spectrum is fitted with three distinct peaks at 398.4, 398.4, and 399.8 eV, representing sp2 -C-N bond in the s triazine ring, three carbon atoms bound to N (N-(C)3), and amino groups with hydrogen atom [-C-N-H-], respectively [29–31]. Similar result is also observed for N 1 s spectrum of sample S1. The high-resolution S 2p spectra of sample S2 and S1 shown in Fig. 5 (d) and 6(d) respectively, show peak at 164.5 eV, corresponding to the S-C bond, which implies the structural incorporation of the sulfur atom in the g-C3N4 lattice. In both the samples, the peak intensity of O 1 s spectra are almost comparable, which excludes the possibility of magnetic effect due to oxygen. We have calculated the atomic percentage of S in the graphitic carbon nitride lattice by Scofield’s Relative sensitivity factor [32]. We have listed the atomic percentage of C, N, O and S in Table 1 . For sample S1 the percentage of S in the lattice is 3.6%, where for sample S2 it is 5.8%. We have also determined the ratio of the atomic percentage of C and N, which agrees with the theoretical value, indicating the formation of graphitic carbon nitride lattice.

To detect the magnetic response in our sample, we have performed the temperature dependent magnetization measurements in our doped sample S1 and S2. The measurement is performed with a Quantum design VSM system in the temperature range from 2 K to 300 K. In both the doped samples S1 and S2, a clear bifurcation in the ZFC and FC data indicates the ferromagnetic ordering in the sample as shown in Fig. 7
(c) and (d). Therefore, it is clear that the substitution of the S atoms in the graphitic carbon nitride lattice induces the overall magnetic property in the 2D system and in this process, it is possible to generate a 2D magnetic g-C3N4 system by controlling the doping concentration in the system. It is seen that for undoped g-C3N4 the nature of the magnetization curve is diamagnetic i.e., no magnetic ordering, as reported earlier [29,33], however, for the S doped g-C3N4 nanosheets, the M − H plots show well-defined hysteresis loops with significant magnetic moments, indicating room temperature ferromagnetism in the 2D system, which is in agreement with the ZFC-FC data shown in Fig. 7 (c) and (d). To understand the effect of substitutional doping of sulfur in the graphitic carbon nitride, we vary the doping concentration of S and the results are shown in Fig. 7 (a) and (b).

The field-dependent magnetization curve of g-C3N4 with 5.8% sulfur doping, shows a hysteresis behavior with a coercivity of 315 Oe at 2 K as shown in inset of Fig. 7 (b). The coercivity values of sample S1 and S2 at different temperatures are listed in Table 2 . The most interesting observation is that after doping S atom, a ferromagnetic ordering is developed in the system with a fairly large magnetic moment although graphitic carbon nitride is diamagnetic in nature. On increasing the temperature, the coercivity slowly decreases with temperature, but interestingly, it persists even upto room temperature in accordance with the M vs. T plot. This effect of room temperature ferromagnetism in our system is due to the substitutional doping of S in the g-C3N4 lattice. In this case, the presence of S atoms promotes the formation of induced spin moments due to its unpaired electron in the p orbital. So, the presence of sulfur stimulates the formation of localized spins in the graphitic carbon lattice. Now, graphitic carbon nitride is a sp2 hybridized structure with delocalized ᴨ electrons in the system. Theoretical analysis shows that the doped S atom injects two electrons in the graphitic carbon nitride conduction band [11,34]. Now the delocalized ᴨ electrons of g-C3N4 itself act as a mediator between the induced spins generated in S atoms due to charge transfer effect. This substitution-generated spin systems interact through ᴨ electrons and generates a stable long-range ferromagnetic ordering in our sample which persists even upto room temperature. In our case, the magnetism retains upto such a high temperature due to pumping the electrons in the g-C3N4 conduction band through S-doping.

The doping concentration greatly influences the magnetic property of the sample. Below and above a certain percentage of doping, the magnetic effect is lower than the maximum value. In case of sample S1, shown in Fig. 7 (a), due to lower S concentration the magnetic ordering is weaker compared to S2. When the doping concentration is below the threshold level, the distance between the spin systems is too large to interact through the delocalized electrons, and they remain as an isolated spin center, and there is no magnetic ordering generated in the system.

Electrical and magnetotransport measurements are carried out in the standard four-probe system in the current in-plane method, where the magnetic field is applied perpendicular to the sample. The pellet sample is prepared under 2 tons of pressure with dimension 6 mm in diameter and 0.8 mm thickness, and the electrode is prepared by copper wire using graphite paste (Alfa Aesar). The temperature-dependent resistivity data of samples S1 and S2 in the temperature range from 4 to 300 K was shown in Fig. 8
(a) and (b), suggesting a semiconducting nature. The resistivity level of the lower S doped sample (S1) is higher than the sample with a higher percentage of S doped sample S2. The higher resistivity in the S1 sample signifies a lower density of states and a stronger localized state. The S atom in the g-C3N4 lattice acts as an n-type donor due to its excess electron compared to a C atom, which increases the number of electrons in the system. Due to this charge transfer effect from S to C, the Fermi level of the system is shifted towards the conduction band. Specifically, the resistivity changes from 72 Ω-cm to 116 Ω-cm due to increase in doping from S1 to S2 at 300 K. The reduction of resistivity with increase in sulfur concentration is attributed to more electrons in the conduction band of g-C3N4. In the case of disordered semiconductor material, the conductivity of charge carriers is controlled by the hopping of electrons between localized states near the Fermi level. We have tested our data with different conduction mechanisms. It is the best fitted with two-dimensional Mott variable range hopping (VRH)
ρ
T
=
ρ 0
e x p
T 0
T
1 3
in the low-temperature region, as shown in the inset of the Fig. 8 (a) and (b) in which
l n ρ
is plotted as a function of
T
-
1 3
, which is consistent with our earlier work [29]. Here
ρ 0
is a constant and
T 0
is the Mott characteristic temperature. The obtained fitting parameters are listed in Table 3 . The high-temperature conduction follows the thermally activated band conduction mechanism [35].

Magnetotransport measurements are performed on samples S1 and S2 from temperature 2 K to 300 K under the magnetic field of 8 T, shown in Fig. 9 . The magnetoresistance of doped graphitic carbon nitride is relatively unexplored. The MR is defined as
MR = [ ρ
H
- ρ
0
/ ρ
0
]
, where H is the applied magnetic field,
ρ
H
resistivity in applied magnetic field and
ρ
0
is the resistivity at zero magnetic field. The magnetoresistance results show an interesting large unsaturated quadratic positive magnetoresistance (150% at 2 K for S1, shown in Fig. 9 (a)) in the low-temperature region for both the samples and a crossover from positive to negative magnetoresistance at temperature 50 K for sample S2, shown in Fig. 9 (c) and (d). Besides this, the room temperature negative MR is also an interesting result for the spintronic application.

The low-temperature positive magnetoresistance in the Mott variable range hopping region is explained by the wave function shrinkage model. Several studies show that positive magnetoresistance for a disordered system is the feature of the variable range hopping mechanism [36,37]. This model demonstrates that in the disordered system under the application of magnetic field, the hopping of electrons is diminished, and an increase of positive MR is observed. In the VRH region, the positive magnetoresistance is defined by the following equation.
(1)

t 2
= 5 / 2016
is a numerical constant,
e
is the electronic charge, N
E F
is the density of state at the Fermi level. The Bohr radius is defined by
a 0
which is similar to the localization length [38,39]. After fitting the MR data with the model, we have estimated the value of localization length (
a 0
) for both the samples at different magnetic field at a particular temperature, as summarized in Table 4 . The localization length is the maximum at low field and is found to decrease with increasing magnetic field. The density of states N

has been calculated from Eq.

(Table 5 ) decreases. The probability of charge carrier hopping between localized states increases with increasing N

The calculated values of
R

at different magnetic field are summarized in Table 4 for both the samples S1 and S2. The values of average hopping length
R
hop
decreases with increasing temperature, listed in Table 5, and as a result, the positive magnetoresistance decreases with increasing temperature. In addition, with increase in temperature, the localized energy states become less localized, and the wave function shrinkage becomes weaker at higher temperature. Therefore, the weak localization effect dominates, and we get a crossover from positive MR to negative MR for sample S2, shown in Fig. 9 (d).

The weak localization effect mainly describes the negative magnetoresistance when the spin–orbit interaction is absent in the system. In weak localization picture, in the presence of a magnetic field, the time-reversal symmetry between two time-reversed paths breaks down. Due to this, the constructive interference between two counter-propagating waves is destroyed and gives rise to negative MR. The magnetoresistance due to the weak localization effect theoretically predicted by Kawabata [40], Altshuler, and Aronov [41], and given by the equation.
(4)
MR
WL
= -
e
2
2
π
2
ħ
e
ħ
1 / 2
H
1 / 2

The MR data indicates that there is a completion between wave function shrinkage and weak localization effect over the whole temperature range. The M − T data show that for sample S2 up to room temperature, ferromagnetic ordering is present in our sample, promoting the weak localization effect over the wave function shrinkage. We get a transition from positive MR to negative MR. For sample S1, the ferromagnetic ordering is weak compared to S2 at room temperature, so the weak localization effect is less effective for sample S1. Due to this, the wave function shrinkage effect is more dominating over the weak localization effect, and we observed a small negative MR in the low field region, shown in Fig. 9 (b).

In summary, we have experimentally shown that S doping in the graphitic carbon nitrite lattice greatly influences the electronic and magnetic properties of the g-C3N4 lattice. Here, sulfur doping in g-C3N4 lattice promotes the formation of induced spin moments in graphitic carbon nitride lattice due to charge transfer effect. These induced spin moments interact through the ᴨ electrons present in the sp2 structure of g-C3N4 lattice. Due to this interaction, we get a long-range ferromagnetic ordering that persists even upto room temperature with a good magnetic moment, and coercivity values, where the pristine graphitic carbon nitride is diamagnetic in nature. Below a threshold level of doped sulfur (<5.8%), the separation between two induced spin moments is large enough to interact through ᴨ electrons, and they behave independently of each other, which significantly influences the magnetic ordering. Here, S atoms act as an n-type donor, which affects the band structure of the graphitic carbon nitride, and we observed an interesting crossover from positive MR to negative MR. Moreover, the room temperature large negative MR makes the system useful for spintronic applications. We believe our results give a detailed insight of magnetism in g-C3N4 lattice and open a new path for the advanced spintronic application.

Chinmoy Majumder: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Tapas Kumar Mondal: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Shatabda Bhattacharya: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Shyamal K Saha: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: C.M. acknowledges Department of Science and Technology (DST)-INSPIRE New Delhi for awarding their fellowships. T.K.M. and S.B would like to thank IACS for awarding Post-doctoral fellowship. We are very thankful to Prof. Dipankar Chakraborty for his valuable suggestions. S.K.S. acknowledges DST, New Delhi, Government of India for the infrastructural facility.
